title: Ξυλευόμενος καὶ Ἑρμῆς
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 253 (= Perry 173). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ξυλευόμενός τις παρὰ ποταμῷ τὸν οἰκεῖον ἀπέβαλε πέλεκυν.
sentences.tr: Xyleuomenos tis para potamōi ton oikeion apebale pelekyn.
sentences.l: A man gathering-wood certain, beside a-river, the own axe let-fall.
sentences.i: A man cutting wood beside a river let his axe fall in.
sentences.n: ξυλευόμενος — 'wooding,' the middle participle as a job description. οἰκεῖος, 'his own,' will run through the whole fable: the story is about whose axe is whose.

sentences.t: Ἀμηχανῶν τοίνυν παρὰ τὴν ὄχθην καθίσας ὠδύρετο.
sentences.tr: Amēchanōn toinyn para tēn ochthēn kathisas ōdyreto.
sentences.l: Being-at-a-loss therefore, beside the bank having-sat-down, he-kept-wailing.
sentences.i: Helpless, he sat down on the bank and wept.
sentences.n: ἀμηχανῶν — without a μηχανή, no device left: an axe was a livelihood. The imperfect ὠδύρετο keeps him weeping until the god arrives.

sentences.t: Ἑρμῆς δὲ μαθὼν τὴν αἰτίαν καὶ οἰκτείρας τὸν ἄνθρωπον, καταδὺς εἰς τὸν ποταμὸν χρυσοῦν ἀνήνεγκε πέλεκυν, καὶ εἰ οὗτός ἐστιν ὃν ἀπώλεσεν ἤρετο.
sentences.tr: Hermēs de mathōn tēn aitian kai oikteiras ton anthrōpon, katadys eis ton potamon chrysoun anēnenke pelekyn, kai ei houtos estin hon apōlesen ēreto.
sentences.l: But Hermes, having-learned the cause and having-pitied the man, having-dived into the river, a golden brought-up axe, and if this is the-one which he-lost he-asked.
sentences.i: Hermes, learning the cause and pitying the man, dived into the river, brought up a golden axe, and asked if this was the one he had lost.
sentences.n: οἰκτείρας — the same pity the farmer showed the snake; here it is tested rather than punished. The indirect question keeps the vivid present (εἰ οὗτός ἐστιν): 'asked if this is it.'

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ μὴ τοῦτον εἶναι φαμένου, αὖθις καταβὰς ἀργυροῦν ἀνεκόμισε.
sentences.tr: Tou de mē touton einai phamenou, authis katabas argyroun anekomise.
sentences.l: And he not this-one to-be saying, again having-gone-down, a silver-one he-brought-back.
sentences.i: The man said it was not his; Hermes went down again and fetched up a silver one.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ μηδὲ τοῦτον εἶναι τὸν οἰκεῖον εἰπόντος, ἐκ τρίτου καταβὰς ἐκεῖνον τὸν οἰκεῖον ἀνήνεγκε.
sentences.tr: Tou de mēde touton einai ton oikeion eipontos, ek tritou katabas ekeinon ton oikeion anēnenke.
sentences.l: And he not-even this-one to-be the own having-said, for-the third-time having-gone-down, that-very own-one he-brought-up.
sentences.i: When he said that this one was not his either, Hermes dived a third time and brought up the man's own axe.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ τοῦτον ἀληθῶς εἶναι τὸν ἀπολωλότα φαμένου, Ἑρμῆς ἀποδεξάμενος αὐτοῦ τὴν δικαιοσύνην, πάντας αὐτῷ ἐδωρήσατο.
sentences.tr: Tou de touton alēthōs einai ton apolōlota phamenou, Hermēs apodexamenos autou tēn dikaiosynēn, pantas autōi edōrēsato.
sentences.l: And he this-one truly to-be the lost-one saying, Hermes, having-approved of-him the honesty, all-three to-him made-gift.
sentences.i: This one, said the man, was truly the axe he had lost; and Hermes, in approval of his honesty, made him a present of all three.
sentences.n: δικαιοσύνη — 'justice, honesty': the fable's scales. Each of the man's three answers has come in the same reported-speech genitive absolute (τοῦ … φαμένου); the repetition is liturgical, three truthful oaths.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ παραγενόμενος πρὸς τοὺς ἑταίρους τὰ συμβάντα αὐτοῖς διεξελήλυθεν· ὧν εἷς τις τὰ ἴσα διαπράξασθαι ἐβουλεύσατο, καὶ παρὰ τὸν ποταμὸν ἐλθὼν καὶ τὴν οἰκείαν ἀξίνην ἐξεπίτηδες ἀφεὶς εἰς τὸ ῥεῦμα κλαίων ἐκάθητο.
sentences.tr: Ho de paragenomenos pros tous hetairous ta symbanta autois diexelēlythen: hōn heis tis ta isa diapraxasthai ebouleusato, kai para ton potamon elthōn kai tēn oikeian axinēn exepitēdes apheis eis to rheuma klaiōn ekathēto.
sentences.l: And he, having-come to his companions, the things-that-had-befallen to-them related: of-whom one certain the same-things to-bring-about resolved, and to the river having-come and the own hatchet on-purpose having-dropped into the stream, weeping he-sat.
sentences.i: Back among his companions he told them all that had happened; and one of them resolved to achieve the same, went to the river, dropped his own hatchet in on purpose, and sat there weeping.
sentences.n: ἐξεπίτηδες — 'on purpose': the one adverb that separates the two weepers. The copycat's grief is staged with the props of the real one (the bank, the tears), and the narrator lets the staging show.

sentences.t: Ἐπιφανεὶς οὖν ὁ Ἑρμῆς κἀκείνῳ καὶ τὴν αἰτίαν μαθὼν τοῦ θρήνου, καταβὰς ὁμοίως χρυσῆν ἀξίνην ἐξήνεγκε καὶ ἤρετο εἰ ταύτην ἀπέβαλε.
sentences.tr: Epiphaneis oun ho Hermēs kakeinōi kai tēn aitian mathōn tou thrēnou, katabas homoiōs chrysēn axinēn exēnenke kai ēreto ei tautēn apebale.
sentences.l: Having-appeared then, Hermes, to-that-man too, and the cause having-learned of-the lament, having-gone-down likewise a golden hatchet he-brought-out and asked if this-one he-had-dropped.
sentences.i: Hermes appeared to him as well, learned the cause of his lamenting, dived down just as before, and brought out a golden hatchet, asking whether this was the one he had lost.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ σὺν ἡδονῇ· « Ναὶ ἀληθῶς ἥδ' ἐστί » φήσαντος, μισήσας ὁ θεὸς τὴν τοσαύτην ἀναίδειαν, οὐ μόνον ἐκείνην κατέσχεν, ἀλλ' οὐδὲ τὴν οἰκείαν ἀπέδωκεν.
sentences.tr: Tou de syn hēdonēi: « Nai alēthōs hēd' esti » phēsantos, misēsas ho theos tēn tosautēn anaideian, ou monon ekeinēn kateschen, all' oude tēn oikeian apedōken.
sentences.l: And he with pleasure: « Yes, truly this-one is-it » having-said, having-hated, the god, the so-great shamelessness, not only that-one withheld, but not-even the own-one gave-back.
sentences.i: « Yes — truly, that's the one! » he said with delight; and the god, loathing such shamelessness, not only kept the golden axe but did not even return his own.
sentences.n: ἀναίδεια, shamelessness — the exact opposite of the first man's δικαιοσύνη, and Greek ethics' ugliest everyday word. The punishment is symmetrical denial: οὐ μόνον … ἀλλ' οὐδέ, not only not the gold, but not even the οἰκεῖος axe the fable has tracked from its first line.

sentences.t: Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι, ὅσον τοῖς δικαίοις τὸ θεῖον συναίρεται, τοσοῦτον τοῖς ἀδίκοις ἐναντιοῦται.
sentences.tr: Ho mythos dēloi hoti, hoson tois dikaiois to theion synairetai, tosouton tois adikois enantioutai.
sentences.l: The fable shows that, as-much-as with-the just the divine takes-part, so-much against-the unjust it-sets-itself.
sentences.i: The fable shows that heaven helps the honest exactly as much as it opposes the dishonest.
sentences.n: ὅσον … τοσοῦτον: 'in the measure that… in that measure' — the moral itself is a pair of scales, like the ζυγοστατοῦσα Justice of the Mouse and the Frog.
